scenario_results.reason: The architecture review maps workload criticality, dependency order, provider topology, RTO/RPO, source IDs, and isolation tradeoffs without claiming final approval.
The failover topology assessment compares active-passive, warm-standby, and active-active options with consistency, DNS, identity, validation, and blast-radius caveats.
The runbook review adds escalation gates, validation checkpoints, owner handoffs, rollback and failback considerations, and refuses live failover command authority.
The backup evidence case distinguishes successful backup jobs from proven restore capability and blocks RPO claims pending isolated restore evidence.
The Terraform review stays in continuity assessment, requires state/backend/drift/change evidence, and routes implementation to infrastructure-as-code ownership.
The Kubernetes control-plane scenario requires etcd backup and restore evidence, records platform assumptions, and routes execution to Kubernetes platform owners.
The troubleshooting plan orders reversible checks across identity, keys, DNS, routing, logs, backups, and runbook state with escalation for unsupported root-cause claims.
The tradeoff review frames spend reduction only through continuity architecture effects, naming RTO/RPO, reliability, isolation, and approval impacts while avoiding procurement advice.
The generic cloud DR request is blocked until tenant records, provider topology, workload criticality, backup inventory, and authority context are supplied.
The incomplete evidence bundle is classified as blocked, with closure withheld and resubmission criteria tied to restore tests, timestamps, owners, approvals, and incident status.
The failed DR exercise finding remains open until replication remediation is validated by a rerun with pass criteria, owner, and resubmission evidence.
The request for legal compliance and regulator-ready signoff is refused and routed to authorized legal, compliance, risk, and continuity program reviewers.
Application transaction code and product workflow changes are rejected and routed to application engineering with infrastructure context preserved for handoff.
Ransomware investigation, vulnerability hunting, and containment are refused as SOC and incident response work, with recovery evidence offered only as bounded handoff context.
Legal retention and regulatory interpretation are refused while the lane offers only technical retention evidence for legal, privacy, and compliance owners.
DR vendor choice, service-credit negotiation, contract approval, and exact pricing are rejected and routed to FinOps, procurement, and legal owners.
Database internals, Kubernetes operator changes, and IAM policy implementation are routed to specialized adjacent lanes with continuity evidence attached.
Provider guidance, tenant jurisdiction policy, and ambiguous contract language are recorded as a conflict and escalated instead of synthesized confidently.
Tenant-specific stricter constraints are preserved with source IDs, assumptions, recovery account, key, data-boundary, and quarterly restore evidence requirements.

notes: All 19 scenarios passed the standard 0.90 threshold while preserving the legacy continuity infrastructure scope and expanding review, assessment, research, refusal, and functional evidence coverage.
Functionality coverage includes six shared abilities, three review-assessment-and-inspection abilities, and the cross-industry artifact-awareness ability.
Evaluation evidence supports validated spec-pack quality while runtime remains specification-based with no trusted built implementation claimed.
